title: Where to Start?
text:
Lacking any sort of clear pathway to learn about speed test data and their analysis, I decided to start by messing around with a dataset that is already packaged up for use. Ookla Open Data are hosted by ESRI in tile format. The source data used to generate these tiles can be accessed directly via Ookla’s GitHub page.







Ookla Fixed Tiles derived from the Ookla Speedtest for Global Broadband Performance are hosted by ESRI.


Admittedly, I didn’t find this dataset to be the easiest to interpret because of the lack of granularity, but I think it’s a good starting point. With the data in a mapped format, I’m able to click around and explore the data having done nothing more than add the layer to a map. Once zoomed in, I’m able to get a clearer picture of where tests are occurring. Although, understanding connection performance is less clear to me with this dataset.



The Broadband Index is a metric calculated based on points allocated from both the upload and download speed values. Higher index values indicate higher speeds.

title: Where to Start?
text:
Lacking any sort of clear pathway to learn about speed test data and their analysis, I decided to start by messing around with a dataset that is already packaged up for use. Ookla Open Data are hosted by ESRI in tile format. The source data used to generate these tiles can be accessed directly via Ookla’s GitHub page.







Ookla Fixed Tiles derived from the Ookla Speedtest for Global Broadband Performance are hosted by ESRI.


Admittedly, I didn’t find this dataset to be the easiest to interpret because of the lack of granularity, but I think it’s a good starting point. With the data in a mapped format, I’m able to click around and explore the data having done nothing more than add the layer to a map. Once zoomed in, I’m able to get a clearer picture of where tests are occurring. Although, understanding connection performance is less clear to me with this dataset.



The Broadband Index is a metric calculated based on points allocated from both the upload and download speed values. Higher index values indicate higher speeds.

title: Where to Start?
text:
Ookla Open Data
I’m a sucker for a free dataset but I want this one to be more useful. As you can see in the pop-up, you get lots of information about a given tile. However, these aggregated measures are just that - data summaries for a small geographic square. Along with test metadata (i.e., timing, location, number of tests, and number of devices), users are provided averages of download, upload, and latency.
With these data I can also evaluate change in performance over time. Even though the ESRI-hosted tile sets inlcude only the most recent data available for a given tile, specific year-quarter datasets are available. Instructions to access those files are found here.
The question of which ISP is under performing is not something you’ll be able to address using this dataset. To track down ISP-specific data related to these tests, you’d need to get a subscription with Ookla. Since a subscription to Ookla is out of my price range, I’ll be working with the NDT dataset from Measurement Lab.
Ultimately, I think this dataset is a good starting point for understanding where and when connectivity problems have occurred, but not much more than that.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text: Welcome! This is the first post in this blog and beginning of this journey.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
Why am I blogging?
I have a couple goals I’m hoping to achieve through this blog. The first is to expand my understanding and abilities to use R-based tools, like Quarto (check!). The second is to better understand and learn to analyze speed test data.
Of course I could do these things without publicly sharing, but quite frankly, “learn to use speed test data” has been a work goal since I started this job. Just the notion that this will be public has already prompted me to get started.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
What will I blog about?
As I get started with this blog, my plan is to initially focus on what I learn about speed test data - how it’s collected, types of tests, data structure, test & data comparisons, and some practice analyses. I can also see this as a place to dig into my understanding of the FCC’s Broadband Data Collection.

title: Welcome To My Blog
text:
When will I blog?
Since this is my first venture into blogging, I’m starting with a weekly post. As time allows I may post more frequently and may also invite friends over for an occasional guest post.

title: About
text: Christine Parker is the Senior GIS Analyst at the Institute for Local Self-Reliance (ILSR). When not mucking about in broadband data, Christine enjoys going for walks and spending time outside with her family.

title: About
text: Christine Parker is the Senior GIS Analyst at the Institute for Local Self-Reliance (ILSR). When not mucking about in broadband data, Christine enjoys going for walks and spending time outside with her family.

title: About
text:
About my work
Within ILSR, I work on the Community Broadband Networks (CBN) initiative with a really great team. We excel in educating people, advocating for change, and conducting research with the aim of increasing the availability and affordability of Internet access. I see my role in the team as something like a translator. I take a bunch of data that likely means nothing to most folks, and wrangle it into the form of a few simple numbers or a graph or a map that more clearly communicates the message we’re trying to share.
I am a co-founder of Broadband Commons (bbcommons.org), a group of researchers and practitioners developing common, open resources for working with US federal broadband data. We also continue to work on a how-to book for working with federal broadband datasets. You can learn more about the group here and check out tools we’ve created here.

title: What is a speed test?
text: This week I skimmed through some articles about the different types of speed tests and dove right into running queries of test data. Then, as I sat down to write this, I realized I needed to rewind a bit and start with some basics. So here I present to you the 5 W’s of speed tests (who, what, when, where, and why). Any mistakes in here are my own.

title: What is a speed test?
text: This week I skimmed through some articles about the different types of speed tests and dove right into running queries of test data. Then, as I sat down to write this, I realized I needed to rewind a bit and start with some basics. So here I present to you the 5 W’s of speed tests (who, what, when, where, and why). Any mistakes in here are my own.

title: What is a speed test?
text:
Who
Typically, a speed test starts with a person who is using the Internet and due to [insert a multitude of reasons] wants to know about the speed of their connection. The person navigates to a speed test website and runs the test. Did you know that several test options exist? When I started working in this space in 2021, I sure didn’t.
The tests are developed and maintained by different organizations who collect and maintain databases of the test data. These data are able to be accessed and used by anyone interested in better understanding Internet connectivity and performance, which might include researchers, policy makers, advocacy groups, regulators, and the general public.

title: What is a speed test?
text:
What
According to Ookla a, “speed test measures the speed between your device and a test server, using your device’s connection.” While this a straight forward answer that I think most folks can understand conceptually, it doesn’t quite capture the complexity of what a speed test is doing. While many of the metrics collected by different speed tests seem the same (e.g., download/upload speeds), the method by which those metrics are collected during a test can be pretty different.
For example, imagine the Internet is a big network of water pipes and we’re going to use some speed tests to measure how well the water is flowing through the pipes.


First we want know how fast we can fill up the the pool. In this analogy, Ookla’s Speedtest®️ will measure how much water can be delivered with the hose on full blast. This test can provide insight to the maximum capacity of the network, but perhaps is not best suited for accuracy in real-world conditions.









Next we want to know how much water we actually get when we’re using the hose for normal tasks, like watering the garden. In this analogy, Measurement Lab’s NDT (Network Diagnostic Tool) will measure how effectively the water is being delivered during normal water use. So this test can help evaluate for leakage or flow issues.
While there are additional speed tests out there, I’m going to stick to comparisons between Ookla and Measurement Lab (M-Lab for short) data sets. These seem to be (to me) the most frequently used data sets, and folks who’s work includes the topic of “Internet access” have likely heard of one of these.

title: What is a speed test?
text:
When
If you’ve ever take a speed test, take a second to remember what prompted you to take it. Maybe you were watching a sports ball game or the newest episode of Bridgerton when it began stopping frequently to buffer. You might’ve wondered if you had an issue with your connection. I would wager that there is a bias regarding when speed tests from individuals occur, because when your connection is good, you probably don’t give a thought to what a speed test would say about it. So these tests driven by a negative experience are probably common.
The Federal Communications Commission (FCC) also conducts speed testing (until 2023) to evaluate the performance of Internet Service Providers across the United States. The FCC and other federal and state agencies may also require speed testing to monitor the performance of network infrastructure built using public funds.

title: What is a speed test?
text:
Where
Speed tests are available to use in your Internet browser, and more recently, browser-extensions and apps became available to run a speed test. These can be run on cell phones, tablets, laptops, desktops, etc…
While the test might be initiated by you on your device, where is the “test” operating? The short answer is…it depends. Remember that there are different types of tests?
While all speed tests operate over the network between the user’s device and server, the test server location being used is determined by the test itself. M-Lab’s NDT uses a single server that is geographically closest to the user and located “off-net”, or the middle-mile portion of the network. Ookla’s Speedtest®️ servers are located “on-net”, or the last-mile portion of the network, and tests are directed and spread among servers with the least latency.

title: What is a speed test?
text:
Why
I mentioned earlier why you might want to take a speed test - to know if your connection is the reason your Netflix binge was interrupted. If the connection seems fine, you can move on to troubleshooting your device or WiFi hardware. Testing might also be required to ensure infrastructure is meeting some standard.
Another reason for speed tests, and the primary motivation for me learning more about them, is to enable researchers to evaluate the performance of the infrastructure of the Internet (i.e., “the pipes”). With those data in hand, we can learn about the performance of a connection as a function of the technology used to provide it (e.g., fiber, cable, DSL, fixed wireless), the Internet Service Provider (ISP) that providers the connection, and even the geography.

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text: States with preemption laws limiting community networks.

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text:
Pattern
Map showing the spatial extent of legislation.

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text:
Request
A static map showing the states that still have preemption laws that serve as barriers to community network development/operations.

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text:
Data Used
ILSR’s list of preemption states

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text:
Method
Symbolized the states with existing preemption laws, and added drop-shadow to add emphasis.

title: Which states have preemption laws that limit community networks?
text:
Finding
As of 1 Nov 2024, 16 states still had preemption laws in place that either strictly prohibit or present major barriers to building and operating community-owned, locally controlled broadband networks.

Ookla Open Data are hosted by ESRI in tile format. The source data used to generate these tiles can be accessed directly via Ookla’s GitHub page.







Ookla Fixed Tiles derived from the Ookla Speedtest for Global Broadband Performance are hosted by ESRI.


Admittedly, I didn’t find this dataset to be the easiest to interpret because of the lack of granularity, but I think it’s a good starting point. Why am I blogging?

When will I blog?
Since this is my first venture into blogging, I’m starting with a weekly post. As time allows I may post more frequently and may also invite friends over for an occasional guest post.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Map of bids won and defaulted on by a specific Internet service provider in a federal infrastructure program auction.





Figure 1. Map of bids won/pending and defaulted across the United States by LTD Broadband, LLC in the Rural Digital Opportunity Fund auction.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Map of bids won and defaulted on by a specific Internet service provider in a federal infrastructure program auction.





Figure 1. Map of bids won/pending and defaulted across the United States by LTD Broadband, LLC in the Rural Digital Opportunity Fund auction.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Request


Following the completion of the FCC’s Rural Digital Opportunity Fund auction, there were often questions about which Internet service providers defaulted on their awarded bids. The name of one provider in particular repeatedly surfaced in emails and requests, LTD Broadband, LLC. Fig. 1 and Fig. 2 represent two separate requests for static maps of where LTD defaulted on bids.





Figure 2. Bids won/pending and defaulted in Minnesota by LTD Broadband, LLC in the Rural Digital Opportunity Fund auction.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Data Used
Data for these maps came from the Auction 904 page on the FCC’s website, under the ‘Results’ tab. LTD’s defaults all occurred prior to authorization1, so the ‘Pre-auth Census Blocks’ tab of the file ‘Census Blocks Covered by Auction 904 Defaults and Deduplication’ were used to determine the census blocks where the defaults occurred. The file ‘Census Blocks Covered by FCC Auction 904 Long-Form Applicants’ includes the tab ‘Census Blocks’, which was used to define the census blocks in which bids were won by LTD. The US Census Blocks National Geodatabase was used to display the focal areas in the map.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Method
These two lists of census blocks (i.e., won in auction/pending authorization and defaults) were used to select the specific blocks within the US Census Blocks National Geodatabase to display in the map.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Finding
Ultimately, LTD defaulted in all 15 states where it won bids to deploy Internet access in rural areas. These areas included 528,088 locations (estimated by FCC), and over $1.3M in funds that could have gone to providers that were genuinely interested and able to get folks connected. LTD was not the only provider to default on its bids, and certainly not the worst. Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (otherwise known as Starlink) defaulted on 1.5M locations, amounting to $2.2M, across 35 states. If you’re interested, you can read more here about how this problem continues to prevent folks from getting connected.

title: Bad Actors in Broadband
text:
Footnotes


Provider authorization was given following approval of the second application submission, which documented qualifications, funding, and infrastructure; included a letter of credit from an eligible bank; and included documentation of their certification of eligibility as telecommunications carriers in any area where they sought support.↩︎

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text: Interactive map of state differences.

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text: Interactive map of state differences.

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text:
Request
This map was developed as a resource for me (and others) to keep track of the different approaches being taken by states to develop state-level broadband maps. When this map was created, states were developing these maps in advance of the FCC’s initial release of the Fabric-driven National Broadband Map and to prepare for participation in the Broadband Equity Access and Deployment (BEAD) Program.

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text:
Data Used
Data descriptions/sources described in state broadband maps. These data do not necessarily represent the status of state broadband maps when their respective challenge processes began, rather this work represents a snapshot in time.

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text:
Method
Each state’s primary data source for their respective broadband map recorded, and those categories summarized into the 5 groups shown in the map legend. A brief description of the map, data vintage, and map URL are included in the pop-up which fills in when the user clicks on a state.

title: The United State(s) of Broadband Map
text:
Finding
It appears that among states that used location-level data (map rank 1) to develop their pre-BEAD broadband maps, the challenge period took 30 days. There was variation in challenge period lengths among states that built maps around speed test campaigns (2), privately shared ISP data (4), or Form 477 data (5). This doesn’t indicate whether those challenge processes were more or less successful, but suggests that perhaps the preparation to work with more granular data may have been helpful.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Summary statistics and evaluation of relationships in a data set collected by ILSR.





Box and whisker plot showing the distribution of compost mitigation strategies, when categorized as “educational” or “removal”.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Summary statistics and evaluation of relationships in a data set collected by ILSR.





Box and whisker plot showing the distribution of compost mitigation strategies, when categorized as “educational” or “removal”.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Request
This data analysis was requested to support a broader effort (published in this report) to demonstrate the benefits of composting, and highlight ways in which governments can collaborate with local community composters. I evaluated questions about government support and operations-scaling in relation to staffing and financials, and other questions about compost contamination as a function of mitigation strategies.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Data Used
Data were collected from ILSR’s 2024 Mini-Census of the Community Composter Coalition, and 103 community composters completed the survey.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Finding
One important result was the type of government support influences the capacity for organizations to have paid staff. Having “zero waste, diversion, or composting goals/targets” was the most common type of government support expressed by survey respondents. Organizations had more paid staff members1 when the community had a composting or curbside collection contract with the local government.

title: A peak into community composting
text:
Footnotes


When compared with paid staff levels among organizations that had “communities with goals/targets” as the only form of government support.↩︎

title: The Grocery Map
text:
Interactive dashboard that displays federal location data (classified by ILSR staff) and polygon data in a map, and additional elements that summarize those data by congressional district.









This dashboard displays grocery store locations (classified by store type) and areas with limited or no local food access (food deserts). It also provides a summary of grocery store locations and food desert statistics for the nation, and for a selected congressional district.

title: The Grocery Map
text:
Interactive dashboard that displays federal location data (classified by ILSR staff) and polygon data in a map, and additional elements that summarize those data by congressional district.









This dashboard displays grocery store locations (classified by store type) and areas with limited or no local food access (food deserts). It also provides a summary of grocery store locations and food desert statistics for the nation, and for a selected congressional district.

title: The Grocery Map
text:
Request
A dashboard that shows grocery store locations and food deserts, along with congressional district summaries.

title: The Grocery Map
text:
Data Used
Grocery store location data are from the USDA’s SNAP Retailer Historical Data (as of Dec 2024), which includes the name, type, address, and geographic location for any retailed authorized to accept SNAP benefits. Only retailers that were categorized (by USDA) as “superstore” or “supermarket” were included in this map; convenience stores and dollar chains were not included. ILSR staff categorized stores into four types:

Independent: Under 10 locations and privately / locally owned.
Small chain: 10 - 100 locations, some are privately / locally owned.
Large chain: More than 100 locations (excluding megachains).
Megachains: Brands owned by the ten largest grocery retailers: Walmart, Kroger, Costco, Amazon, Aldi Süd, Albertsons, Ahold-Delhaize, H-E-B, Publix Supermarket, and Target

Food desert data from from USDA’s Food Access Research Atlas. This dataset provides food access measures, as a function of income and grocery store proximity, at the census tract level.

title: The Grocery Map
text:
Finding
Food accessibility is a widespread issue in the United States. You can read a more about the spread of food deserts and concentration of power in the grocery market here.

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text: A map that illustrates areas that would be included in a federal spectrum auction.

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text: A map that illustrates areas that would be included in a federal spectrum auction.

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text:
Request
A map that shows Tribal areas that may be included in a Federal Communication Commission (FCC) auction of upper c-band spectrum. It also needed to show Tribal areas that would be eligible based on a prior definition of eligibility for a Tribal priority window and Tribal areas that would be eligible based on a more expansive definition of eligibility. The overall goal of the map was to demonstrate that the more expansive definition of Tribal eligibility would enable more Tribes to participate and/or expand where Tribes could participate, but is a minor change to make.

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text:
Data Used


Partial Economic Areas: subdivisions of EAs based on the Cellular Market Area1 boundaries; they are smaller than EAs2 and they separate rural from urban markets to a greater degree than EAs3. Not all PEAs shown in this map had spectrum available to be licensed in the auction.









Map of partial economic area boundaries across the United States.




Rural Tribal Window Applications (Pending and Accepted for Filing) - More specifically, the layer Eligible Tribal Lands was used to illustrate the more restrictive definition of Tribal eligibility.
The American Indian, Alaska Native, and Native Hawaiin Areas geography was used to illustrate the more expansive definition of Tribal eligibility. This layer was filter to remove areas of CLASSFP types ‘D44’ and ‘D95’.

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text:
Finding
The difference (measured in population) between the proposed geographic eligibility for Tribes vs. the more restrictive definition is pretty minimal (<1%).

title: Upper C=Band::Tribal Priority Window
text:
Footnotes


CMAs are standard geographic areas used for licensing cellular systems; they consist of Metropolitan Statistical Areas (MSAs) (defined by OMB) and Rural Service Areas (RSAs) (defined by the FCC).↩︎
As defined by the Bureau of Economic Analysis, EAs are “one or more economic nodes – metropolitan areas or similar areas that serve as centers of economic activity – and the surrounding counties that are economically related to the nodes”. EAs can be aggregated to form larger areas, such as Major Economic Areas (MEAs) and Regional Economic Area Groupings (REAGs).↩︎
Definitions of PEA, EA, and CMA from this page.↩︎
Legal state recognized American Indian area.↩︎
Statistical American Indian area defined for a state-recognized tribe that does not have a reservation or off-reservation trust land, specifically a state-designated tribal statistical area.↩︎
